species: Peregrine Falcon stoop through tern colony -- Falco peregrinus (Falconidae) diving through breeding colonial Sternidae alarm event
scientific_name: Falco peregrinus (Peregrine Falcon, Family Falconidae) stooping through breeding colony of Sterna paradisaea (Arctic Tern) and/or Sterna hirundo (Common Tern) and/or Hydroprogne caspia (Caspian Tern). Multi-species predation event. Pacific Northwest coastal cliff and seabird colony.
sound_type: Two-phase predation event: (Phase 1) Peregrine Falcon stoop at 240-390 km/h -- audible air rush/whoosh as falcon passes (broadband 100 Hz - several kHz, directional, <1 second duration) preceding impact; (Phase 2) Tern colony alarm explosion: simultaneous onset across entire colony within 1-2 seconds of predator detection. Individual Arctic/Common Tern alarm: 5,000-12,000 Hz, 200-500 m individual carry. Colony of 500-10,000 breeding pairs alarming simultaneously: acoustic saturation of 3,000-12,000 Hz frequency range across entire colony spatial footprint (potentially 200-500 m diameter). Mobbing response: individual terns pursuing Peregrine, adding wing-noise broadband mid-frequency (500-5,000 Hz) and continuous alarm vocalizations during pursuit. Total event duration: 30 seconds - 5+ minutes depending on number of stoop attempts. ACOUSTIC TRANSITION from colony baseline E=5-6 to alarm E=9 in 1-2 seconds. The fastest natural transition in SPS acoustic history.
ecosystem: Coastal cliff breeding context: Peregrine Falcon nesting on cliff face adjacent to or above colonial seabird nesting area (beach, rocky island, gravel bar, sea stack). Pacific Northwest coast: Flattery Rocks NWR, Cape Flattery, Protection Island NWR, Dungeness NWR, San Juan Islands. Cliff face provides Peregrine with altitude advantage for stoop initiation, unobstructed flight path to colony, and escape route post-strike.
season: Summer -- breeding season. Colony at maximum density: adults on nests, chicks in various developmental stages. Alarm response is maximum during breeding season when colonial defense is strongest and chick survival is at stake. Peregrine breeding season overlaps with tern colony breeding season; predation pressure is highest when prey concentration is highest.
family: Multi-species: Falconidae (Falco peregrinus) as predator; Sternidae (Sterna paradisaea, Sterna hirundo, Hydroprogne caspia) as prey-colony. Interaction is the predation event; the acoustic event is triggered by the interaction.
milestone: THE RAMP RISES TO E=9. Third step of SPS final ascent (341 E=8 → 342 E=8 → 343 E=9 → 344 E=10). E=9 through predation-event acoustic explosion: the fastest natural transition in SPS history (colony baseline E=5-6 → colony alarm E=9 in 1-2 seconds). The triggered transition -- not sustained maximum -- is the E=9 mechanism. Paired with Blood Brothers metal (S36-343, E=9): both reach E=9 through explosive simultaneous multi-source vocalization. E=18 combined.

coordinates.description: Representative: Flattery Rocks / Cape Flattery area, Clallam County WA. 48.38°N 124.72°W. Primary coastal cliff Peregrine habitat and adjacent colonial seabird nesting. Protection Island NWR (48.13°N 122.92°W, Jefferson County): largest seabird nesting colony on WA coast, Peregrine documented predation. Dungeness NWR (48.12°N 123.18°W): Common Tern colony and Peregrine documentation. San Juan Islands (48.5°N 123.0°W): multiple colonial nesting and Peregrine predation sites.

acoustic_data.stoop_sound: Falcon air rush: broadband 100 Hz - several kHz, directional, <1 second. Audible at close range (10-50 m) as a sharp whoosh or roar of disturbed atmosphere. Not as acoustically powerful as the colony response but provides directionality cue. Maximum stoop velocity: 240-390 km/h (documented maximum 389 km/h, Ken Franklin 2005).
acoustic_data.colony_alarm: Individual tern alarm: 5,000-12,000 Hz peak, 2-4 second duration per call, 200-500 m individual carry. Colony alarm cascade: begins 1-3 individuals near perceived threat, cascades to full colony within 1-2 seconds. 500-10,000 breeding pairs simultaneously: spectral saturation 3,000-12,000 Hz, spatial distribution up to 500 m diameter, temporal onset simultaneous. Acoustic transition from baseline E=5-6 to alarm E=9: 1-2 seconds. Fastest acoustic transition in SPS engine.
acoustic_data.mobbing_sound: Individual terns in pursuit: alarm calls continuous at 5,000-12,000 Hz during pursuit; wing-beat noise at 500-2,000 Hz as multiple birds power-fly at maximum speed. Mobbing can continue for minutes if Peregrine makes multiple passes or remains in colony airspace. Extended event duration vs. single stoop.
acoustic_data.e9_mechanism: E=9 through triggered acoustic explosion: the predation event transitions the colony from baseline E=5-6 to alarm E=9 in 1-2 seconds. Contrast with: SPS 341 E=8 (gradual three-stream convergence); SPS 342 E=8 (sustained collective assertion); SPS 340 E=9 (concentrated behavioral apex from single source). The 343 mechanism is unique: the fastest transition from low energy to maximum energy through a biological triggering event.

nodes.title: Predation Event as Acoustic Trigger -- Fastest Transition in SPS
nodes.description: The Peregrine stoop through the tern colony transitions the colony from baseline E=5-6 to alarm E=9 in 1-2 seconds. No other SPS entry has this speed of energy transition. The triggering event (stoop) is acoustically distinct from the triggered event (colony alarm). E=9 from the second phase.

nodes.title: Peregrine Stoop -- Fastest Animal Movement on Earth
nodes.description: 240-390 km/h maximum stoop velocity. Wing-folded body position, nictitating membrane, nasal baffles. Audible air rush <1 second at close range. Impact: closed-foot strike or talon rake at maximum approach speed. The fastest predatory event in the animal kingdom.

nodes.title: Tern Colony Alarm Explosion -- Simultaneous 500-10,000 Pair Response
nodes.description: Colony alarm begins 1-3 individuals, cascades to full colony within 1-2 seconds. 500-10,000 pairs simultaneously: 5,000-12,000 Hz saturation across colony spatial footprint. Mobbing: aerial pursuit of Peregrine extends event. One of the most acoustically dramatic events on the Pacific Northwest coast.

nodes.title: Final Ascent Step 3 -- E=9 RISES
nodes.description: SPS ramp: 341 E=8 (convergence) → 342 E=8 (colony assertion) → 343 E=9 (predation explosion) → 344 E=10 (imagined synthesis). The ramp lifts from E=8 to E=9 here. One step from the historic E=10.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. Both reach E=9 through explosive simultaneous multi-source vocalization. Blood Brothers: dual screaming at maximum from the first bar. Tern colony: simultaneous alarm from 500-10,000 breeding pairs triggered in 1-2 seconds. The Blood Brothers sound like the inside of a tern colony when the Peregrine stoops.

E=8 rises to E=9. Sea lion colony: sustained E=8 through continuous collective assertion. Tern colony: E=9 through instantaneous triggered explosion. Continuous maximum vs. explosive triggered transition as the difference between E=8 and E=9 at these entries.

E=9 rises to E=10. The predation explosion at 343 is the most intense single-event drama in the SPS engine before 344. The imagined synthesis at 344 exceeds it by incorporating all SPS categories simultaneously.
